Nimble, compact, and relatively obedient, although not always on their best behaviour.
Delivery robots like these have become increasingly common on UK streets over the past decade.
From Leeds to Milton Keynes, the machines bring online orders of food and goods right to your door at top speeds of four miles per hour.
Companies behind the technology say robots can be faster, safer, and better for the environment than deliveries by road.
But for some locals living in the trial areas, they can be a bit of a nuisance.
I find them quite menacing.
There's already a lot going on.
The last thing you need is a robot zooming towards you quite fast.
They're just quite handy, aren't they?
It's quite a new little thing, a niche thing for Leeds to have.
I don't like speaking to people, so it's a good way to not have to speak to anyone.
50% of the street is automated machines, and half of it is people trying to just get around them.
I'm like, sorry, somebody squeezed past your robot.
It's not...
it's our street.
It's our street, for humans.
A government consultation into whether to bot or not is underway.
There's currently no national framework for the use of autonomous delivery robots.
But ministers are considering legalising their use across the UK.
Stop it, please.
I'm at work and I don't want to be late.
Some charities are concerned about how this could impact vulnerable road users.
Excuse me, would you please let me pass?
We think the people worst affected are those who are visually impaired, wheelchair users, and then parents pushing prams.
Particularly speaking about disabled people, of which many of them tend to be elderly,
we actually know that obstructed pavements lead to people making less walking journeys, which can breed social isolation.
Starship Technologies, the market leader in robot deliveries, says it puts lived experience of disability at the heart of what it does and works with disability charities to improve designs.
And the Department of Transport says whilst it welcomes innovation and advances in technology, it's vital the safety of pedestrians and vulnerable users is put first.
But with more than 10 million humanless deliveries already completed worldwide, Has the robo-takeover already begun?
Katie Roberts, BBC News, the robo-takeover.

We're joined now by Lisa Johnson, who's vice president at Starship Technologies, which designs and operates delivery robots, and also James Barber, the interim chief executive at the organisation Transport for All.
And I think we've got another guest as well who's joining us this morning.
I believe so.
Here we go.
Making his entrance, navigating the ramp.
The suspense, there we go, perfect parking.
Good morning to you too.
Good morning.
Good morning to the robot.
I said good morning.
Good morning, John and Sarah.
It's great to be on BBC Breakfast.
It's great to have you too, Mr. Robot.
Thank you.
I feel like I'm tomorrow as well.
They'll play back these clips in years to come, remember when this was a novelty.
Lisa, I mean, there's an astonishing figure at the end of Katie's piece there, that there are already millions of these deliveries have already happened around the world.
Absolutely.
Starship have done more than nearly 2 million in the UK, 10 million globally.
So I know people think this is kind of the thing of the future, but actually for a bunch of communities, this is kind of just normal life now.
And that's when We know we're kind of winning is when it goes from being a novelty, and people going, "Oh, hey, there's a robot," to it just being a part of normal.
Yeah, because I live in Bristol, and I've seen them on the streets now and again in Bristol.
That's one of the places where it's been trialled.
But where else have you been doing it in the UK?
That's right, so Bristol, they're not actually our robots.
In Bristol we are in Milton Keynes, Northamptonshire, West Yorkshire, just down the road actually in Greater Manchester
in Sale, Wakefield, a bunch of places across the UK where it is kind of just normality now.
And what do people get delivered?
What do they use them for?
So the top delivery in the UK is actually milk, so quite often it's top-up shopping.
So if you think about how deliveries have changed and grocery shopping's changed over the last...
Decade or so, people increasingly want small amounts of goods.
They want it quickly, and that demand has just exploded.
So the question is, how do you meet that need that people have?
And so that's what the robots, that's the gap in the market really that the robots fill.
So quite often it's milk, bananas.
It's that top-up shopping when you've got the kids home from school, you've not got everything that you need for tea, so you get the robot to bring it for you.
So James, they're filling a gap, but they're also filling the pavements, aren't they?
And for that reason, you have some concerns?
Yeah, so at Transport for All, we are a national disability organisation representing the disabled community, and one of our key issues is around the regulation.
So at the moment, there is a lack of regulation around delivery robots, and as a result, we have some real concerns around safety.
Our members and disabled people across the United Kingdom are saying that, ultimately, these are delivery robots that are taking up large spaces on already congested pavements,
and we feel very strongly that disabled people, and indeed everyone, should own the pavements, and people should always come before delivery robots and other potential barriers that could arise.
I mean, is there a balance, presumably, in the same way that people who aren't maybe as capable of walking, actually having that as a way to deliver to them, that— Is helpful as well.
There is a balance there, is there?
Absolutely, and we fully accept that there are also some benefits to them.
Many disabled people will have different needs, so there are potential benefits that may arise from that, but for us, those benefits don't always outweigh the harms.
When we have people with, say, sight impairments who are walking along long pavements which are already quite congested as it is,
they are raising serious concerns that these robots might not be able to pick up their supports.
Their mobility aids, and ultimately it can cause them quite serious injury if something goes wrong.
I think you have trained it, Lisa, haven't you, so that if the robot comes into conflict or gets stuck with a mobility device, it can send out a message.
I think we can get a demonstration of that now.
Let's have a listen.
"Hello, we seem to be in a tricky situation."
Don't worry, I've asked for a human to help remotely, so you can be on your way as soon as possible.
It should only take a few seconds.
So how does that actually work when it's asking a human?
Is there a human tracking the movements of the robot at all times?
So the robot can call for help.
They're 99% autonomous, so whenever you see them out in the wild, they're navigating on their own.
But in those rare occasions where they're like, actually, I don't know what.
to do here, or maybe I need a little bit of help, they'll just call a remote human who'll go, "OK, I see what's happening.
I'm going to give you a little hand to help you out."
And so last year, for example, in the operating areas we've got in the UK, we identified around 23,000 mobility devices or wheelchairs.
There were very, very few incidents where we had to call someone to help the robot move or reverse, because it's scanning the whole time.
So A bit like a person walking down a street, you can see if people are coming towards you, cars moving in the periphery.
The robot's got a 360 view of the world, so it kind of does have eyes in the back of its head, and it can anticipate those problems before they happen.
But if we do get into that situation, The robot can call for help and resolve it as quickly as possible.
It's fascinating, isn't it, to think that this could become normal.
We will follow the debate and see how your campaign goes as well.
thank you very much indeed for coming in, all of you.
And they always say, don't they, that robots can take your job one day.
Absolutely.
Surely, robot, that can't happen.
It's time to get the news, travel, and weather where you are.
